text: There are several ways of devascularizing the uterus during a laparoscopic myomectomy. They are injecting vasopressin and ligating the uterine arteries at its origin. In some cases, injection of vasopressin may be contraindicated. In some countries, vasopressin is banned. In open myomectomy, the commonest way of devascularization of the uterus is by using a Foley's catheter as a tonic A. Can a tonic A be applied during laparoscopic myomectomy?

This is a twenty-seven year old lady who had a mid-trimester miscarriage six months ago. Ultrasound showed a large fundal intramural fibroid measuring five point two six times five point two five centimeter and another small subserous fibroid. She underwent a laparoscopic myomectomy.

A Foley catheter is introduced into the abdomen through the ten millimeter camera port and placed around the isthmus of the uterus. The catheter is tied, and a proline suture on a straight needle is used to maintain tension on the knot by passing through the skin and into the catheter twice before being brought out of the abdomen. This procedure is repeated on the left side.

The surgeon pulls on the proline sutures to apply tension on the catheter, effectively devascularizing the uterus, which appears white at the fundus. A transverse incision is made on the uterus, and the fibroid is enucleated with minimal bleeding observed.
The surgeon removes the posterior subserous fibroid and closes the defect in the uterine wall using a barb suture, ensuring the tissue edges are approximated securely.

The surgeon closes the serosa using interrupted polyglycine I sutures. The proline suture is cut, and the catheter is removed, resulting in bleeding from the second incision. The surgeon then sutures the second incision with figure of eight sutures to control the bleeding.
Anti-adhesion gel is applied over the suture lines to prevent postoperative adhesions.
